summary: Fowler reflects on the evolution of reuse in object-oriented programming, from individual classes to frameworks, and introduces the concept of 'seedwork' as an alternative. While frameworks provide part-built applications you extend in controlled ways, seedworks offer minimal functionality you copy and modify freely. Though this copy-paste approach contradicts conventional wisdom about reuse, Fowler argues seedworks may be more practical because good frameworks are extremely hard to create. He concludes that while reuse within an application is vital, reuse across applications faces fundamental social and technical barriers that make imperfect alternatives worth considering.
key_points: The promise of OO reuse evolved from classes to frameworks, with mixed results especially on the business side
Seedworks offer minimal functionality you copy and modify freely, trading upgradeability for simplicity and ownership
Good frameworks are very hard to create; seedworks are 'not as useful but easier to create and use'
Shared library versioning remains an unsolved problem (DLL-hell, dependency conflicts)
Application boundaries are primarily social constructions, making cross-application reuse fundamentally harder than within-application reuse
money_quotes: A framework is supposed to be a part-baked application that you extend in controlled ways to provide what you need. A seedwork is some minimal functionality that you modify however you like to get what you need.
Seedworks are not as useful as a good framework, but are easier to create and use. The point is not whether they are ideal, but just whether they are useful.

I've found that reuse (or avoiding duplication) within an application is vital. But reuse across applications is much tougher, primarily because an ApplicationBoundary is primarily a social construction.
This is the kind of copy and paste reuse that many people, including me, deride. Maybe I shouldn't be so scornful.
We still haven't really figured out how to deal with shared libraries that upgrade on different schedules.
key_insight: The pragmatic value of reuse depends on context—perfect reuse across application boundaries faces social barriers that make simpler, 'imperfect' approaches like seedworks worth considering.
